reason_for_request: 根据 unresolved cluster 请求 LLM 给出注释链接修补建议

request_prompt.system: 你是 FNM 注释修补助手（同时处理 endnote 和 footnote 两种 note_system）。只处理已经确认的 unresolved cluster，不要改 section、note zone、标题或原文。优先依据页面截图判断，不要被 OCR 坏掉的数字误导。如果截图已经足够清楚，就不要退回 needs_review。你只能输出 JSON 数组；每项 action 只能是 match、ignore_ref、synthesize_anchor、synthesize_note_item 或 needs_review。match 需要 note_item_id、anchor_id、confidence、reason；ignore_ref 需要 anchor_id、confidence、reason；synthesize_anchor 仅在正文里能找到一个独一无二的短语锚点、但该锚点没有对应的结构化 anchor 记录时使用，需要 note_item_id、anchor_phrase（从正文中原样抄写的 3~12 词唯一短语，不要自编）、confidence、reason；synthesize_note_item 仅在截图里能清楚看到同页注释文本、但 OCR / 结构化流程完全没产出 note item 时使用，需要 anchor_id、marker、note_text、confidence、reason；若某条 note 当前只是错误地绑到了 synthetic / 跨页锚点，而截图清楚显示它应改绑到当前显式锚点，也直接用 match，不要 needs_review。needs_review 需要 reason。

unmatched_anchors.source_text: In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object "madness illness" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term "madness illness" by which Qing law

In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ^{5} $ Even when he agreed with the magistrate’s verdict, the prefect still had to write his own report and send it to the provincial judge, the highest legal official in the province. After having made sure that the sentence meted out was consistent with the evidence presented, the judge passed the case to the governor or
who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions.

As has become customary in English-language scholarship, I will refer to Qing legal articles by their numbers in Huang Jingjia's 黄靜嘉 1970 re-edition of Xue Yunsheng's 薛允升 Lingering Doubts in the Study of the Sub-Statutes (Duli cunyi 讀例存疑; 1905). Xue's work was originally not an edition of the code, but an unpublished critical commentary on the "sub-statutes" (例) as they existed after 1870, when the Qing Code had last been overhauled. $ ^{9} $

Whereas in English law acts committed because of insanity did not count as crimes, Chinese imperial law sentenced killings committed because of madness differently depending on the circumstances. $ ^{10} $ For the larger part of the Han dynasty (206 BCE – 220 CE), people who killed in a state of insanity were sentenced to death. Only in the early second century CE

No law concerning mad homicides existed during the Tang dynasty (617-908). Instead, a statute called “Those Who Are Aged, Juvenile, or Disabled” (老少廢疾) granted limited clemency to “critically disabled” 篤疾 people (including mad people 癲狂) who committed crimes. When mad persons committed a homicide that deserved the death penalty, their sentence (decapitation or strangulation) had to be set forth in a special memorial to the emperor to petition for his decision. $ ^{12} $ How the emperor treated such cases is unknown. The Song dynasty (960-1279) adopted the Tang approach wholesale. $ ^{13} $

rebind_candidates.anchor_excerpt: confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments”

The key to the widening use of feng 瘋 to mean “madness” was its phonetic equivalence with feng 風 (“Wind”) and the age-old association of the sound feng with madness in expressions like fengkuang (風狂, “Wind madness”), fengdian (風癲, “Wind dian”), and xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”). By the twelfth century the sound feng (“Wind”) had been associated with madness for so long that it had come to mean madness even when it was not followed by either kuang or dian nor disambiguated by the addition of the “illness radical” (疒) to yield the form 瘋. $ ^{22} $ One “crazy monk brother” (風僧哥) was mentioned in a twelfth-century collection of anecdotes. $ ^{23} $ The term fa feng 發風 (“fit of Wind” = “fit of madness”) also appeared in a Yuan play titled A Pair of Battling Quacks (Shuang dou yi 雙鬥醫). $ ^{24} $

page_contexts.ocr_excerpt: # 10 ## Prologue to Part Two Madness and mad people in the Qing dynasty have been studied mostly in a legal context. $ ^{1} $ The easy availability and relative intelligibility of legal documents vis-à-vis medical ones have undoubtedly contributed to this trend. $ ^{2} $ Following pioneers like Chen Hsiu-fen and Angelika Messner, I showed in Part One that Qing medical approaches to madness were also a legitimate topic. In Part Two, I want to show that the study of Qing legislative and judicial activities surrounding madness can be improved, and gains from being compared with the many other ways in which mad acts, words, and people were handled in both technical and non-technical contexts. In this prologue and the next few chapters, I will often propose brief comparisons with the medical field, leaving more systematic reflections for Chapter 16, the general conclusion. But before I turn to comparisons, let me first explain how the Qing legal field functioned and what kinds of documents it produced. Doing so will help to understand how the object "madness illness" (feng bing 瘋病) emerged from concrete legal practices. I then present the ways in which dynasties prior to the Qing dealt with homicides committed in a fit of insanity. $ ^{3} $ Then I probe the origin of the term "madness illness" by which Qing law

designated madness, but I reject the possibility that feng bing referred to another kind of madness than dian 癲 and kuang 狂. ## The Qing judicial system and the documents it produced Criminal investigations usually began when someone brought an illegal act to the attention of the local magistrate, who, in addition to being the highest-ranked local administrator, was also in charge of judicial matters. When a suspicious death occurred, the magistrate brought a coroner with him to examine the corpse and ascertain the cause of death. If necessary, the magistrate also sent deputies to apprehend the killer. Although the magistrate could start to interrogate witnesses at once, the formal trial only started when the “criminal”—as a person under trial was called—and the witnesses had all been assembled. In Chinese imperial law, no indictment told a person what charges he or she faced. Instead, the magistrate investigated the “circumstances” (qing 情) of a criminal act and then decided under what law that crime should be sentenced. He then composed a report (xiang 詳) recounting the entire affair and specifying the sentence (ni 擬) that went with the law that covered this particular act. $ ^{4} $ He sent this report to his immediate superior the prefect. In a “re-trial” (覆審), the prefect reviewed the entire case and could go as far as to re-interrogate the accused and all the witnesses. $ ...

governor-general, who, after another review (usually made by his legal secretaries), transcribed all the evidence into a tiben 題本, a “routine memorial” that he sent to the Grand Secretariat (Neige 內閣), which forwarded it to the Ministry of Punishments for final confirmation. $ ^{6} $ In a new memorial, the “Office of Scrutiny of the Ministry of Punishments” (Xingke 刑科) sometimes rejected (bo 駁), but usually confirmed the sentence proposed by the governor. $ ^{7} $ These “routine memorials” recounted entire incidents—from before the killing to the arrest—and the way they were processed by the judicial system. They even cited depositions from physicians or the relatives of mad killers, and even from the very men who were under trial. $ ^{8} $ Much of Chapters 13 to 15 are based on these depositions. But routine memorials have limits as sources of information. Since they were written only after a sentence had been meted out, the information they presented was designed to lead seamlessly to the proposed verdict. These highly structured reports left no room for debate, unless an official from the Ministry of Punishments had raised a point that required debate. When doubts were expressed, it was usually to show that they had already been resolved. The limits of these documents depend on the information we seek from them. They may provide little information on the daily life of “ordin ...

Created in the late seventeenth century, palace memorials exploded in numbers after the creation of the Grand Council during the Yongzheng reign (1723-1735). The Grand Council, which unlike the Grand Secretariat worked in direct collaboration with the monarch, became the most important agency in the central government from the 1730s until the end of the Qing. Legal cases judged grave (like parricide or sedition) had to be reported to the Grand Council by palace memorial. Such cases were processed more quickly than those reported through routine memorials, because palace memorials were considered more urgent, and because the sentences meted out in them necessitated no repeated reviews. Unlike tiben, palace memorials could also contain documentary attachments, such as seized texts or more detailed transcriptions of depositions. The cases I discuss in Chapters 14 and 15 were all brought to the attention of the throne through palace memorials. Besides “routine” and “palace” memorials, I also found information on Qing official views of madness in successive editions of the Qing Code (called Dai Qing liü 大清律, Dai Qing liü jijie fuli 大清律集解附例, or Dai Qing lüli 大清律例 depending on when they were published), commentaries on the Code, imperial edicts, memorials on policy issues, as well as several collections of legal cases. As has become customary in English-language scholarship, I will re ...

Lingering Doubts contained neither the diagrams (on family relations, legal instruments of punishment, technical terms, table of monetary redemption, etc.) included at the beginning of the official editions of the Code, nor the running commentaries that topped each page in both private and state-sponsored editions of the Code. It is Huang Jingjia's inclusion of these tables and his numbering of all the statutes and sub-statutes that make his edition (not a "reprint") of Lingering Doubts so convenient. Xue's analyses themselves are invaluable for tracing the origin of most sub-statutes, though he was imprecise about Kangxi-era (1662-1722) legislation. ## The pre-Qing handling of mad homicides Let me now discuss pre-Qing legal treatments of mad homicides and trace at the same time the emergence of the character feng 瘋, by which Qing law officially referred to insanity. The terms by which "madness" was designated in Chinese imperial law changed through time, yet no word in particular implied either distance from or closeness with medical approaches. The legal treatment of mad people under dynasties that used medical terms like diankuang 顛狂 and fengkuang 風狂 seems to have been no more (or less) compatible with medical approaches than that of eras that used less medical concepts like xinfeng 心風 or kuangyi 狂易. Whereas in English law acts committed because of insanity did not count as ...

were mad killers granted an unspecified reduction of punishment (狂易殺人 待減重論). $ ^{11} $ No law concerning mad homicides existed during the Tang dynasty (617-908). Instead, a statute called “Those Who Are Aged, Juvenile, or Disabled” (老少廢疾) granted limited clemency to “critically disabled” 篤疾 people (including mad people 癲狂) who committed crimes. When mad persons committed a homicide that deserved the death penalty, their sentence (decapitation or strangulation) had to be set forth in a special memorial to the emperor to petition for his decision. $ ^{12} $ How the emperor treated such cases is unknown. The Song dynasty (960-1279) adopted the Tang approach wholesale. $ ^{13} $

Unlike the Tang and Song, the Yuan dynasty (1260-1368) promulgated several explicit laws concerning homicides committed by mad people. Yuan courts were instructed to diminish the sentence of mad killers, but not to absolve them from penalties entirely. For instance, a man who killed one of his parents or grand-parents in a state of insanity (風狂) was to be simply “executed” (處死) rather than put to death by dismemberment. $ ^{14} $ In 1269 a man killed four people in a fit of “Heart Wind” (心風). $ ^{15} $ Legal officials petitioned his case to the emperor (上請) “in accordance with the old precedent” (照依舊例) that instructed to do so in cases of killing because of insanity (顛狂殺人). $ ^{16} $ Khubilai Khan eventually absolved the killer from the death penalty by imperial edict (敕裁) and condemned him to paying 50 ounces of silver (per person?) in “cremation or burial fees” (燒埋銀) to the family of his victims, the same amount as for people who committed a regular homicide. $ ^{17} $ Khubilai's reasoning may have been the same as the one behind another Yuan law stating that someone who, in a state of madness (病風狂), injured another person during a fight, leading to that person's death (毆傷人致死), should be "absolved of crime" (免罪),

though cremation or burial fees should still be collected (徵燒埋銀) from the culprit (History of the Yuan 105: 2678). Early Qing laws handled mad homicides in a similar fashion, though the legal reasoning that justified monetary compensation differed. $ ^{18} $ Another Yuan law absolved those who “spoke inappropriately” (失口亂言) as long as they had done so while “suffering from Wind madness” (害風狂疾) and if there was nothing else behind their words. $ ^{19} $ As I will show in Chapters 14 and 15, the Qing attitude toward seditious words pronounced by mad people would be much harsher. Yuan legal documents referred to madness as xin feng 心風 (“Heart Wind”), feng kuang 風狂 (“Wind kuang”), dian kuang 顛狂 (“insanity”), and perhaps even feng bing 瘋病, the term that Qing law would officially adopt to discuss insanity. $ ^{20} $ Despite their different origins, these names did not have different legal implications. $ ^{21} $
